messages.author: Steve M6IBU
messages.text: Not a pet pic but I'm after some advice. I have a ridgeback / mastiff cross, he's a lovely boy, coming up to 8 years old on Christmas Day actually... the past couple of years every few months he decides to vigorously scratch himself raw normally in one spot. When it has happened before, I clean it with diluted Hibiscrub (recommended by local pet shop), use clippers to get the hair down and then sudocrem (also recommended) the area. Then put a cone collar on him. Normally after 2 weeks it's almost completely cleared up and new hair has grown. Within a month he's fine again. This time he's done it really badly in 2 places and I'm really worried there's a deeper cause. He's well loved and looked after, never left alone, walked daily, a generally very happy dog. I am going to see the vet today but I wondered if anyone has any advice for me before I take him? I did consider some doggy boots on his paws to make sure that even if he tries to ruck at his bad places he can't hurt himself... any thoughts very welcome... (edited)
messages.content_html: Not a pet pic but I'm after some advice. I have a ridgeback / mastiff cross, he's a lovely boy, coming up to 8 years old on Christmas Day actually... the past couple of years every few months he decides to vigorously scratch himself raw normally in one spot. When it has happened before, I clean it with diluted Hibiscrub (recommended by local pet shop), use clippers to get the hair down and then sudocrem (also recommended) the area. Then put a cone collar on him. Normally after 2 weeks it's almost completely cleared up and new hair has grown. Within a month he's fine again. This time he's done it really badly in 2 places and I'm really worried there's a deeper cause. He's well loved and looked after, never left alone, walked daily, a generally very happy dog. I am going to see the vet today but I wondered if anyone has any advice for me before I take him? I did consider some doggy boots on his paws to make sure that even if he tries to ruck at his bad places he can't hurt himself... any thoughts very welcome... (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: therps2 🇺🇸
messages.text: Good on you! Gawd, I hope he makes it! I've rescued an owl and a golden eagle before. Fortunately, both survived and were released.

messages.author: K7
messages.text: The vet called me, they said he’s not doing very well but he is alive and they are working on him. It sounds like they’re going to let me be a part of the release, which is cool.

messages.author: therps2 🇺🇸
messages.text: That would be very cool. I was present for the eagle's release, but not the owl. It's awesome to think you had a (very) small part in that release. I'm hoping he'll do well and be released. If not, perhaps he can be an "ambassador" animal so schoolkids can learn about them. That's important, too.

messages.author: therps2 🇺🇸
messages.text: As I've often said, unless you get a tortoise or a parrot...the odds are that you're going to outlive your pet. It's a painful truth. On an aside about the allotment kitty: I had a half-moon parrot named "Percy". He was "Percy" for 15 years...then tried to lay an egg. Turns out, it was a "Pricilla". I guess the yolk was on us.
messages.content_html: As I've often said, unless you get a tortoise or a parrot...the odds are that you're going to outlive your pet. It's a painful truth. On an aside about the allotment kitty: I had a half-moon parrot named "Percy". He was "Percy" for 15 years...then tried to lay an egg. Turns out, it was a "Pricilla". I guess the yolk was on us.
